items.q: What is the main advantage of asymmetric encryption?
items.correct: Secure key exchange without sharing a secret in advance.
items.misconception: It is faster than symmetric encryption.
items.why: Students think asymmetric is just 'better'. It's actually SLOWER than symmetric. Its advantage is solving key distribution - the public key can be shared openly without compromising security.

items.correct: The ability of different classes to respond to the same method call in their own way.
items.misconception: Having multiple classes with the same name.
items.random_wrong: Variables that change type at runtime.
items.why: Students confuse polymorphism with naming. Polymorphism means 'many forms' - a single interface (method signature) behaving differently depending on the object's actual class.

items.q: What is abstraction in computer science?
items.correct: Removing unnecessary detail to focus on essential features of a problem.
items.misconception: Making something abstract art or vague.
items.random_wrong: Writing code that does not work.
items.why: Students borrow the everyday meaning of 'vague'. In CS, abstraction is a precise technique: ignoring details that don't matter for the current task, while keeping what does.
The answer examiners want is: Microsoft Word. “Linux kernel” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Has visible source code. “Always free of charge” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many main categories of software are taught at GCSE (system and application)?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 categories. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
